output_text:
Summary: Reviewed the introduction; found one fatal methodological problem, several factual/positioning claims that need verification or revision, and weak baseline framing. Recommendations follow, ordered by severity.

## Critical issues

**1. The baseline comparison is methodologically broken.**
You say you "use [Carrasco-Davis et al. 2019's] reported numbers as the reference point against which routing-system gains are measured," and then claim "a 1.4x improvement in cost-adjusted F1 ... on the held-out Rubin sample." Carrasco-Davis et al. (2019) was evaluated on HiTS, not Rubin. Reported F1 numbers from one survey, taxonomy, and cadence cannot be subtracted from numbers on a different survey to yield a "gain." You must re-run the baseline classifier on the ALERT-Bench evaluation splits (both ZTF and Rubin) and compare like-to-like. As written, reviewers will reject this as an unsupported headline number.

**2. Choosing the 2019 paper over the 2021 follow-up is backwards.**
The stated reason — "the five-class taxonomy aligns more naturally with ALERT-Bench's evaluation grid" — implies the evaluation grid was designed to match the older baseline rather than the more relevant one. Since the 2021 paper is "generalized ... to ZTF-like cadences" and ALERT-Bench draws on 2.4M ZTF alerts, the 2021 model is the natural comparison. Either (a) use 2021 as primary baseline and report 2019 as supplementary, or (b) report both and let the evaluation grid expand to seven classes. The current framing reads as baseline-shopping.

**3. "First benchmark to jointly evaluate classification accuracy and routing cost" — verify.**
Prior work that should at minimum be cited and distinguished: PLAsTiCC (Kessler et al. 2019), ELAsTiCC (Rubin/LSST DESC), and broker-side challenges from ANTARES / Fink / ALeRCE. Some of these touch on triage/routing or computational budget. The "first" claim is defensible only with an explicit definition of "routing cost" and a one-sentence delta vs. each of these. Right now "prior work is dominated by [a single 2019 paper]" is not credible.

## Substantive issues

**4. Define "cost-adjusted F1" before claiming a 1.4× improvement.** F1 has no standard cost adjustment. Either name the metric (e.g., cost-weighted macro-F1 with weights $c_i$) and forward-reference its definition, or use a different headline number. Unitless ratios on undefined metrics are a red flag in benchmark papers.

**5. The ZTF half of the benchmark disappears in the contributions.** You introduce ALERT-Bench as ZTF + Rubin, then the headline result is Rubin-only. Either report both, or explain why Rubin is the headline split (e.g., it's the harder OOD evaluation).

**6. Verify "Rubin commissioning data release."** As of May 2026, confirm what is publicly released and citable; if you are using internal or restricted data, say so and describe access. If you mean DP0/DP1-style precursor data, name it.

**7. "Carrasco-Davis et al. (2021)" — verify it exists and the description is accurate.** I cannot confirm a 2021 follow-up with the specific properties you describe. Double-check the citation; if it doesn't exist, this paragraph collapses.

**8. "Deep recurrent classifier" understates the 2019 architecture.** Carrasco-Davis et al. (2019) used a CNN-RNN over image sequences, not a pure recurrent model. Tighten to "convolutional-recurrent."

## Minor

**9.** "10^5 to 10^7 events per night" — ZTF and ATLAS are ~10^5–10^6; LSST predictions are ~10^7. Consider stating per-survey numbers to avoid the impression that all three sit at 10^7.

**10.** "ensemble routing systems that dispatch each alert to a specialized downstream classifier" — this is a mixture-of-experts setup, not really an "ensemble" (which usually implies aggregating multiple predictions). Use "routing" or "mixture-of-experts" consistently and drop "ensemble."

**11.** "tied to the broker community" is vague — name the brokers (ALeRCE, Fink, ANTARES, Lasair) you've coordinated with, or soften the claim.

**12.** Final paragraph is filler. Either cut it or make it concrete (e.g., "we will extend ALERT-Bench with Year-1 LSST alerts when DR1 is released").

## Suggested rewrite of the baseline paragraph

> We re-evaluate the convolutional-recurrent classifier of Carrasco-Davis et al. (2021) — the most recent published model trained on ZTF-cadence light curves — on the ALERT-Bench splits, and report its performance as our primary baseline. We additionally re-implement the 2019 HiTS model for the five-class subset of our taxonomy. Both baselines are evaluated end-to-end on ALERT-Bench rather than imported from the original papers, since the original training sets, taxonomies, and cadences differ from ours.

## Uncertainty disclosure

I have not independently verified (a) the existence and contents of "Carrasco-Davis et al. 2021," (b) whether a Rubin commissioning data release is publicly available as of 2026-05, or (c) the precise contribution boundaries of ELAsTiCC and broker challenges with respect to routing-cost evaluation. Recommendations 3, 6, and 7 are flagged for you to confirm against primary sources before revising.
